coredata.dc:title: Highly transparent photoelectrochromic device based on carbon quantum dots sensitized photoanode
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Kui
Luo, Gui
Liu, Jian
Zheng, Jianming
Xu, Chunye
coredata.dc:description: The combination of dye or quantum dots sensitized solar cell and electrochromic device has led to the realization of the photoelectrochromic device (PECD). In this study, a PECD consisted of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized photoanode, poly(3,4-(2,2-dimethylpropylenedioxy)thiophene) (PProDOT-Me2) electrochromic film, Br-/Br3 - with Li+ as electrolyte was fabricated, and it shows advantages of highly transparency for bleached state when compared with conventional photoelectrochromic device. As a PECD, it exhibits a reversible optical modulation under constant illumination (100 mW cm−2) by simply altering its circuit states between on and off. A high optical window of 54.6% with coloration time of 4.9 s, and bleaching time of 5.2 s was achieved at 580 nm. Additionally, stable switching for transmittance modulation after 400 cycles demonstrates its good cycle durability, exhibiting great potential in developing energy-saving applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochromic
Carbon quantum dots
Highly transparent
Poly(3,4-(2,2-dimethylpropylenedioxy)thiophene)

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Issue of the IME-13 conference Chiba 2018

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure of the assembled PECD (from top to bottom): FTO glass substrate, TiO2/CQDs PA, electrolyte, EC film and ITO glass substrate.
(a) XRD patterns: FTO glass substrate, pristine TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs films; (b) FT-IR spectra: CQDs, TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs.
SEM images of (a) TiO2, (b) TiO2 /CQDs films. Low resolution TEM images of (c) TiO2 nanopaticles, (d) TiO2/CQDs nanopaticles. High resolution TEM images of (e) TiO2 and (f) TiO2/CQDs nanopaticles.
Schematic diagram of the PECD: (a) band diagram view; (b) in short-circuit condition; (c) in open-circuit condition.
(a) Normalized absorbance and (b) transmittance spectra of the CQDs sensitized photoanode.

Transmittance modulation of the PECD in bleached and colored states from 380 nm to 700 nm, the solid line represent the initial transmittance modulation, the dash line represent the transmittance modulation after 400 cycles.

(a) Transient transmittance response time; (b) transmittance cycle stability; (c) Nyquist plot data of PECD for initial time (black dot line) and after 400 cycles (red dot line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Photocoloration efficiency versus exposure time of the PECD under AM 1.5 G illumination at 100 mW cm−2.
I-V characteristics of the PECD under AM 1.5 illumination at 100 mW cm−2.
Optical performance of some dye or quantum dots sensitized PECD reported in the literatures.

head.ce:title: Highly transparent photoelectrochromic device based on carbon quantum dots sensitized photoanode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale, CAS Key Laboratory of Soft Matter Chemistry, Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale, CAS Key Laboratory of Soft Matter Chemistry, Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of dye or quantum dots sensitized solar cell and electrochromic device has led to the realization of the photoelectrochromic device (PECD). In this study, a PECD consisted of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized photoanode, poly(3,4-(2,2-dimethylpropylenedioxy)thiophene) (PProDOT-Me2) electrochromic film, Br-/Br3 - with Li+ as electrolyte was fabricated, and it shows advantages of highly transparency for bleached state when compared with conventional photoelectrochromic device. As a PECD, it exhibits a reversible optical modulation under constant illumination (100 mW cm−2) by simply altering its circuit states between on and off. A high optical window of 54.6% with coloration time of 4.9 s, and bleaching time of 5.2 s was achieved at 580 nm. Additionally, stable switching for transmittance modulation after 400 cycles demonstrates its good cycle durability, exhibiting great potential in developing energy-saving applications.

Photoelectrochromic device based on carbon quantum dots was fabricated, achieving high optical window of 54.6% at 580 nm.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: An electrochromic device (ECD) is composed of two electrochromic (EC) films, which are able to switch their color reversibly under the application of a suitable voltage [1–5]. Another technology in rapid rise is dye- or quantum dots-sensitized solar cells. The combination of these two technologies has led to the realization of photoelectrochromic device (PECD), whose anode film is replaced by a layer of dye- or quantum dots-sensitized photoanode (PA) [6–8]. In such devices, PA harvests the light and converts it to electric power for driving EC material into colored state. Thus, it has promising applications in energy-saving area. Recently, PAs sensitized with organic dyes have attracted extensive attention. The first dye sensitized PECD developed by Bechinger showed a transmittance modulation of 18% [9]. Ho et al. steadily improved it to 33.7% at 620 nm using EC polymer film [10]. Our group has optimized it to 44% at 582 nm based on the Br-/Br3 - redox couple [11]. It is regretful that the low transmittance of these PECDs in original state have restricted the pursuit of higher modulation. To address this dilemma, some relative transparent quantum dots (QDs) sensitized PAs have appeared as candidates. Deepa first demonstrated a quantum dots sensitized PECD, which were made up by CdS quantum dots sensitized PA, S2-/Sn 2- redox couple and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxypyrrole) hybrid with vanadium pentoxide nanobelt film, presenting optical contrast of 55% at 650 nm [12]. Another design by this group was prepared a half self-powered ECD, with MoO3 film as the cathode, and it presented a maximum optical contrast of 31% at 630 nm [8]. Almost at the same time, our group reported a similar PECD by employing WO3 as the cathode, and a high optical contrast was achieved, but it took more than half an hour for the bleaching process [13].

It is well established that the most commonly used Ru-dyes and metal chalcogenide QDs are not only of high cost and toxicity, but also complicated in film preparation. Aiming to solve these problems, it is urgent to develop a cost-effective, non-toxic and easy-to-prepare photosensitizer. The emerging carbon quantum dots (CQDs) appear to be an ideal alternative to QDs-based photovoltaic devices for their advantages of low-cost, easy synthesis, environmental friendliness and most importantly, the same quantum confinement effect as the metal chalcogenide QDs [14–17]. Hence, efforts have been made in the preparation of CQDs based photovoltaic devices. Kang et al. introduced the CQDs as an electron transfer intermediary into the dye-sensitized solar cell, developing a highly-efficient power conversion system [18]. Li et al. grew CQDs on TiO2 film in situ and improved the power conversion efficiency to 0.87% [19]. Titirici et al. used three kind of CQDs sensitized ZnO nanorods, allowing their use in solid-state nanostructure solar cells [20].

Among all the reported PECD, optical performance of dye or quantum dots sensitized PECD reported in the literatures are summarized in Table 1. It should be noted that rare work has been reported on the preparation of CQDs sensitized PECD. Thus, we here report a newly-developed PECD consisting of CQDs sensitized photoanode, PProDOT-Me2 polymer film as EC electrode and Br-/Br3 - with Li+ as electrolyte. The results showed that the newly-developed PECD achieved an excellent optical contrast of 54.6% at 580 nm. This satisfactory performance is indeed benefitted from the collaboration of highly transparent CQDs sensitized PA and PProDOT-Me2 EC film. The EC properties of this PECD were systematically investigated, including transmittance modulation, response time and long-term stability. Besides, the degradation mechanism of the PECD was also analyzed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

Tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, 99.5%), LiBr (99%), liquid Br2 (99.5%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Acetonitrile (ACN, 99.8%), propylene carbonate (PC, 99.5%), acetone (99.9%), ethanol (99.8%), maleic anhydride (99%), carbamide (99%) were all from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. Monomer 3,4-(2,2-dimethylpropylenedioxy)thiophene was synthesized according to the reported literature [21].

TiO2 paste was prepared according to a previously reported method [22]. Briefly, 10 ml TBT was injected into 20 ml stirred acetic acid, followed by dropping of 10 ml DI water, along with the solution changing from muddy to clear. After stirring for another 3 h, the sol was transferred into an autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 4 h. Then a white paste was obtained and stirred for 2 h before use. Using the doctor-blade method, TiO2 films were coated on the FTO (13 Ω) substrates with the white paste. After drying for 10 min in the air, the films were annealed at 450 °C for 30 min.

Bottom-up strategy was used to prepare the CQDs [23]. In a typical procedure, 2 g maleic anhydride and 2 g carbamide were dissolved in 26 ml ethanol with vigorous stirring and then the mixture was transferred into autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained solution was diluted with 50 ml DI water and filtered with a 200 nm membrane filter. The as-prepared CQDs solution was diluted 5 times in acetone, then TiO2 films were immersed into the solution for 2 h and followed by being washed with ethanol and dried in air.

PProDOT-Me2 films were deposited on ITO (7.8 Ω) glass substrates via electropolymerization method. Constant potential of 1.65 V (vs. Ag/ Ag+) was applied on ITO glass for 10 s with standard three-electrode system (CHI 660D, CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) in a precursor solution that contained 0.1 M LiClO4 and 0.01 M ProDOT-Me2 monomer in ACN. Finally, EC films were obtained kept in nitrogen atmosphere.
Br-/Br3 - electrolyte was prepared in glove box with nitrogen atmosphere by dissolving 2 mM Br2 and 0.3 M LiBr in PC.

PECD was assembled as the traditional sandwich model and the structure was shown in Fig. 1. TiO2/CQDs and PProDOT-Me2 films were chosen as the photoanode and cathode, respectively. The two electrodes were placed face to face with 200 µm silicon oxide spacer and sealed with UV curing sealant and then Br-/Br3 - electrolyte was injected into the spacer. Finally, the injection hole was also sealed and the device was successfully fabricated.

The crystal structures of both films were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, X′Pert PRO; PANalytical B. V., Almelo, Netherlands) with Cu-Kα as the X-ray source. Infrared (IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 8700 Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. Surface morphology of films were determined by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-6700M, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2011, Japan). Transmittance spectra of PA and TiO2 films were measured by an UV–Visible-NIR spectrophotometer (V-670, Jasco, Japan). Transmittance spectra of PECD was measured by a portable spectrometer (USB 2000+, Ocean optics, USA). The incident light intensity was measured by an SCR-300 Solar Tester (Bunkoukeiki Co., Ltd Japan).

The crystal structure of the prepared TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs films were characterized by X-ray diffraction and the results are displayed in Fig. 2a. For pristine TiO2 film, the peaks at 25.39°, 37.77° and 48.09° indicate the anatase (JCPDS 84–1286) phase of TiO2. The TiO2/CQDs film exhibits the same characteristic diffraction peaks as the pristine TiO2 film, implying that the TiO2 has retained its crystallinity after the addition of CQDs. As for the XRD patterns for solid CQDs (see in the Fig. S1), only a broad band centered around 25° was existed, suggesting low crystallinity of the prepared CQDs. Thus, no signal of CQDs can be detected may due to its small amount, high dispersion and low crystallinity of the CQDs [24].

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were further used to examined the presence of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs hybrid films, the curves are shown in Fig. 2b. For the individual CQDs, a broad absorption peak at 3100–3500 cm−1 is attributed to O-H and N-H groups in CQDs [25]. Besides, the absorption peaks at 1645 cm−1 and 1408 cm−1 are corresponded to stretching vibration of C˭O and C-N, respectively. The results indicate the presence of amide, hydroxy and carbonyl group on the surface of the CQDs. In terms of TiO2/CQDs composite, almost all the characteristic peaks of the CQDs could be found in the TiO2/CQDs composite. In addition, comparing with the solid CQDs, the intensity of the characteristic peak of C˭O has been reduced, suggesting the formation of O˭C-O-Ti bond and confirming the strong interaction between the CQDs and TiO2 [26].

Morphologies of the electrodes were observed by SEM and TEM images. Typical SEM images of pristine TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs films are shown in Fig. 3a and b, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 3a, pristine TiO2 nanopaticles present a spherical shape and uniform size, with the rough surface providing sufficient space for the depositing of CQDs. For TiO2/CQDs film, CQDs can hardly distinguished through the SEM images due to their small size. The powders scraped from these films were investigated by TEM to elucidate the elaborate structure of TiO2/CQDs nanopaticles. In Fig. 3c, spherical TiO2 nanopaticles can be seen with a diameter of 30–50 nm. While in Fig. 3d, a large number of CQDs are attached on the edge of TiO2 nanopaticles, which is a convincing evidence that CQDs are loaded on the surface of TiO2 nanopaticles. To clearly observe the structure of the nanopaticles, high resolution TEM images were acquired. As displayed in Fig. 3e, a regular lattice spacing of TiO2 was found to be 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (101) plane of the anatase TiO2 crystal. While in Fig. 3f, the surface of the TiO2 is covered by small amount of CQDs, with the majority of the CQDs falling within the range from 3 to 5 nm in diameter. The individual CQDs were displayed in Fig. S2 with the high resolution TEM images. No obvious crystal lattice can be observed, which fits the result of XRD patterns.

The working mechanism of the developed PECD is similar to that of quantum dots sensitized solar cell. As for quantum dots sensitized solar cell, when the QDs were subjected to band gap excitation under illumination, the electron-hole pairs would formed in the QDs. The induced electrons would injected into the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 and then transferred to the counter electrode through the external circuit. The excited QDs are recovered by the redox couple in the electrolyte and the oxide species of the redox couple were regenerated at the counter electrode [27]. In terms of PECD, schematic diagrams in Fig. 4 were used to clearly illustrate the working mechanism. As presented in Fig. 4a, the photo-induced electrons from excited CQDs injected into the CB of TiO2 under AM 1.5 illumination in short-circuit and immediately transfer to PProDOT-Me2 film across external circuit accompanied with the Li+ intercalation, turning the film from colorless to deep purple. While in the PA, the present Br- in electrolyte could precisely neutralize the excited CQDs and produces Br3 - for oxidized species. Once in the open-circuit condition (Fig. 4c), the electrons could not be delivered to EC film and accumulated in PA, at the same time, the oxide species captured electrons from EC film with Li+ back to electrolyte, resulting in the bleaching of the EC film. These two transition can be achieved reversibly by simply altering its circuit state under constant illumination.

The UV–Vis absorbance and transmittance measurement of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were applied to prove their highly transparency and the results are presented in Fig. 5. The same spectra of the CQDs dissolved in acetone are also presented in Fig. S3. A high absorbance intensity and low transparency were manifested of the CQDs as it's grey color. However, in Fig. 5a, only a slight red shift occurs at the absorbance wavelength between pristine TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs films due to the small amount of CQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 5b, pristine TiO2 film shows a transmittance of over 85% and TiO2/CQDs films can reach nearly 80% in visible light range, which meets the requirement of high performance PECDs.

The transmittance modulation of the PECD in visible range was investigated under an illumination of 100 mW cm−2. Fig. 6 displays the transmittance spectra of the PECD (active area of 2 cm2) for bleached and colored states from 380 nm to 700 nm. The inset graphs represent corresponding images of the bleached and colored states, respectively. For the initial cycles, the transmittance at 580 nm is 67.1% in open-circuit and changes to 12.5% in short-circuit. The curves reflect a typical EC behavior of PProDOT-Me2 film, showing a largest transmittance variation of 54.6% at 580 nm. Undoubtedly, the highly transparent CQDs sensitized PA play a significant role in this satisfactory results. After 400 cycles under illumination, no obvious degradation was found for transmittance modulation at 580 nm, implying a robust cycle stability of the PECD.

The response time is another important parameter for evaluating the EC properties. It was generally defined as the time it takes to reach 95% of the maximum transmittance modulation at certain wavelength. Here, response measurement was carried out by adjusting the circuits on and off for 20 s automatically under illumination at 580 nm. As seen in Fig. 7a, the response time was ca. 4.9 s for coloring process, while took 5.2 s for bleaching process. After 400 cycles under illumination (Fig. 7b), no significant degradation was found in the response time of coloration process since the PProDOT-Me2 EC film possessed a high coloration efficiency of over 200 cm2 C−1. In comparison, recover time of the bleaching process is increased dramatically. This may ascribed to the acknowledged reason for I- or Br- based electrolyte corroding towards the QDs-based photoanode [28–30]. To verify the explanation, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the PECD was conducted for initial time and after 400 cycles under AM 1.5 illumination. As shown in Fig. 7c, the two Nyquist plots can be divided into three semicircles at high frequency, middle frequency and low frequency, which correspond to the charge transfer impedance at EC electrode (Rct1, the inset enlarge graph), charge transfer impedance at PA electrode (Rct2) and finite Warburg impedance (Rw) of Br- electrolyte, respectively [31]. By fitting the Nyquist plots to an reported eletrochemical model, values of Rct1 and Rct2 were obtained as 52 Ω and 119 Ω and they increased to 1212 Ω and 180 Ω after 400 cycles [32]. This result implies that the concentration of Br3 - is insufficient after the PA corroded, thus leading to a reduction in the duration of bleaching process.

Photocoloration efficiency (PCE) is a distinct property for PECD. It can be defined as the PECD performance per unit light intensity illuminated per minute or second, as expressed by:
T
lum
= ∫ f
λ
T
( λ )
d λ / f
λ
d λ
where
f
(
λ
)
being the relative sensitivity of human eye in photopic state and
T ( λ )
presents the measured transmittance spectra of the PECD.

Forthermore:
PCE
lum
=
Δ OD
/
lum
G
T
t =
log
(
T
lum , bleached
/ T
lum , colored
)
/ G
T
t
(
cm
2
s
- 1
W
- 1
)
where T
lum, bleached and T
lum, colored represents the luminous transmittance spectrum in bleached and colored states, respectively, ΔOD
lum means optical density during the coloration, G
T for the normal incident irradiance to the PECD and t for the exposure time (in seconds). For fully colored PECD, T
lum varies from 67.1% to 12.5%, which corresponds a contrast ratio of 5.37:1, appearing a excellent performance. Forthermore, the PCElum value of the PECD was calculated in the coloration process and the results shown in Fig. 8. It can be observed that the highest value occurs in the first 2 s and present a gradual drop thereafter, indicating this PECD undergoes rapid coloration process in a relative short exposure time.

As a semi solar cell, I-V characteristic is essential for the PECD. It was measured under AM 1.5 illumination at 100 mW cm−2 with the active area of 2 cm2, the curve are presented in Fig. 9. The photovoltaic conversion efficiency is η = 0.110124% with the short-circuit current Jsc= 0.285 mA cm−2 and the open circuit voltage Voc= 0.708 V. this result indicated the device performed commonly in photoelectric conversion compared with conventional solar cells. This may be caused by: (i) Most of the photocarriers generated in the PA are consumed in photoelectrochromic reactions, resulting in poor regeneration of the oxidized redox species in the cathode; (ii) low light absorption of the PA electrode for achieving the high transparency of QDs-PECD; (iii) photo-induced electrons may recombine with the hole or capturing by oxide species of the redox couple due to narrow band gap of the CQDs, thus leading to a unfavourable photocurrent. In spite of the generally behavior as a solar cell, this device remained excellent photoelectrochromic properties.

In this work, a PECD was fabricated based on CQDs sensitized photoanode with PProDOT-Me2 EC film and it's excellent EC properties were revealed. The transmittance of the PECD in bleached states was found to be benefitted from the transparency of the photoanode and the device achieved largest transmittance modulation of 54.6% in visible range. In addition, the Br-/Br3 - couple facilitated to attain relatively fast response time. After 400 cycles, the response time of the bleaching process fell off since the concentration of Br3 - fell well short of demand at the surface of PProDOT-Me2 film, resulting in degradation of the PECD. Also, it was found that values of Jsc= 0.285 mA cm−2 and Voc= 0.708 V were sufficient to achieve a short EC response time for PProDOT-Me2 material. Thereby, PECD developed in this research may have a great potential in the application of smart windows, displays and transparent photovoltaic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51503193 and 21774114) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFA0403403).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.solmat.2019.01.004.

Taya, Enhanced contrast ratios and rapidswitching color-changeable devices based on poly(3,4-propylenedioxythiophene) derivative and counterelectrode, in: Proceedings of the Smart Structures and Materials 2002: Electroactive Polymer Actuators and Devices, 4695, 2002, pp. 442–450.
